region_of_origin: Asia (likely Northeast India or China); now cultivated worldwide

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium lemon (approximately 84 g) is a typical single serving, yielding about 3–4 tablespoons of juice.

nutrient_source.title: Lemon, raw, without peel — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167747); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and micronutrients sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 167747 (lemon, raw, without peel). Energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron values are consistent with the input HPB record (energy 29 kcal, protein 1.1 g, fat 0.3 g, carbohydrate 9.32 g, sugar 2.5 g, sodium 2 mg, fibre 2.8 g, calcium 26 mg, iron 0.6 mg). Additional micronutrients (potassium, vitamin C, saturated fat, etc.) sourced from USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Lemon GI is widely cited as approximately 20–25 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). A value of 25 is used here as a conservative upper-bound estimate consistent with published citrus fruit GI data. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (25 × 9.32 × 0.84) / 100 ≈ 2.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium (138 mg/100 g) content supporting DASH dietary targets
hypertension.guidance: Lemon is an excellent choice for individuals with hypertension: its negligible sodium content (2 mg/100 g) and potassium supply (138 mg/100 g) align with DASH diet principles, and its vitamin C and flavonoid content may contribute to endothelial function; use fresh lemon juice as a salt substitute in cooking to further reduce dietary sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25), low sugar (2.5 g/100 g), and soluble fibre (2.8 g/100 g) supporting postprandial glucose control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lemon is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI (~25), low sugar content (2.5 g/100 g), and dietary fibre (2.8 g/100 g) which slows glucose absorption; citric acid may additionally attenuate postprandial glycaemic responses, consistent with ADA dietary guidance — however, lemon-flavoured beverages and desserts with added sugar should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in vitamin C (53 mg/100 g) and flavonoids (hesperidin, eriocitrin) with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory cardiovascular benefits
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lemon provides 53 mg vitamin C per 100 g — a potent antioxidant that reduces LDL oxidation and supports vascular integrity — along with citrus flavonoids (hesperidin, eriocitrin) that have demonstrated lipid-lowering and anti-inflammatory effects in clinical studies, making lemon a cardioprotective addition to a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C content and anti-inflammatory citrus flavonoids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lemon's high vitamin C content (~53 mg/100 g) supports collagen synthesis and acts as an antioxidant reducing oxidative stress in rheumatoid arthritis; its flavonoids (hesperidin, eriocitrin) provide additional anti-inflammatory polyphenol activity consistent with dietary recommendations for RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lemon (Citrus limon) is a small oval yellow citrus fruit with a bright, intensely sour juice, fragrant zest, and edible seeds. It is consumed raw and is rarely eaten whole; instead, its juice, zest, and pulp are used extensively as a flavouring agent in cooking, baking, beverages, and condiments worldwide. Nutritionally, lemon is a low-calorie fruit (29 kcal/100 g) with a low glycemic index (~25), making it suitable for weight management and blood glucose control. It is an excellent source of vitamin C (53 mg/100 g), providing over 50% of the adult daily requirement per 100 g, which supports immune function, collagen synthesis, and antioxidant defence. Its dietary fibre content (2.8 g/100 g) supports digestive health and glycaemic modulation. Lemon is extremely low in sodium (2 mg/100 g) and provides potassium (138 mg/100 g), making it compatible with DASH dietary patterns for hypertension management. Clinically, lemon is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. Its citrus flavonoids — particularly hesperidin and eriocitrin — have demonstrated anti-inflammatory, lipid-lowering, and endothelial-protective effects in peer-reviewed research. Lemon is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. It is used globally across cuisines as a souring agent, garnish, and natural preservative.
common_user_queries: Can I eat lemon if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in a lemon?
Is lemon juice good for weight loss?
What is the glycemic index of lemon?
Is lemon good for heart health?
How much vitamin C is in a lemon?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database (input record, confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 167747 (lemon, raw, without peel). Both sources are approved primary sources. Values are consistent across sources. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008). Ontology codes are defensible from FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy 29 kcal, protein 1.1 g, fat 0.3 g, carbohydrate 9.32 g) are non-zero and sourced from HPB Singapore / USDA SR Legacy; no zero-macro justification required. 2. Saturated fat (0.04 g), monounsaturated fat (0.01 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.09 g), trans fat (0.0 g confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy), cholesterol (0.0 g confirmed absent in plant food), vitamin B12 (0.0 mcg confirmed absent in plant food), and vitamin D (0.0 mcg confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy) are set to 0.0 as confirmed values from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 167747. 3. No per_100g fields are set to -1; all micronutrients have been populated from USDA SR Legacy. 4. GI value of 25 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database citrus fruit data and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care; no single exact lemon GI test is universally standardised, but the range 20–25 is consistently reported. Glycemic load per serving (84 g medium lemon) = (25 × 7.83) / 100 ≈ 2.0. 5. Lemon is a globally consumed fruit with no single cuisine of origin; multilingual names provided for Singapore-relevant languages (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the input record originates from HPB Singapore. 6. FoodEx2 code A000KL and FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 are defensible matches for whole lemon; SNOMED CT code 256329004 is the substance-level code for lemon.
